model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With only 256 optimizer steps, the run currently sees too little of the 50k-example training set. Doubling batch size should expose substantially more unique examples within the same step budget, and a small LR increase keeps AdamW updates appropriately scaled for the larger batch.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last change suggests this workload is strongly limited by data exposure under the fixed 256-step budget. Moving to batch 256 should let training cover essentially the full CIFAR-10 set within budget, and scaling LR upward keeps AdamW from becoming too conservative at the larger batch.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest improvements so far have come from increasing data exposure per fixed optimizer step. Batch 512 should let the 256-step run cover the dataset multiple times while keeping the script simple, and a modest LR increase matches the larger effective batch so optimization does not become too slow.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"After increasing effective data coverage, the next likely limiter is overconfident fitting. Label smoothing is a minimal regularization change that often improves validation cross-entropy directly, which matches the benchmark objective more closely than accuracy alone.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Label smoothing moved validation loss in the wrong direction, so the simplest high-confidence next step is to remove it and recover the stronger unsmoothed objective before exploring other axes.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    warmup_steps = max(1, total_steps // 20)","old":"    warmup_steps = max(1, total_steps // 10)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    warmup = optim.lr_scheduler.LinearLR(optimizer, start_factor=0.5, total_iters=warmup_steps)","old":"    warmup = optim.lr_scheduler.LinearLR(optimizer, start_factor=0.2, total_iters=warmup_steps)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"With only 256 total steps, the current schedule may be spending too much of the run at unnecessarily low learning rates. Shortening warmup and starting closer to the target LR should let the model exploit the strong large-batch AdamW setting earlier without adding architectural complexity or runtime cost.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    warmup_steps = max(1, total_steps // 10)","old":"    warmup_steps = max(1, total_steps // 20)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    warmup = optim.lr_scheduler.LinearLR(optimizer, start_factor=0.2, total_iters=warmup_steps)","old":"    warmup = optim.lr_scheduler.LinearLR(optimizer, start_factor=0.5, total_iters=warmup_steps)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The shorter, hotter warmup degraded validation loss, so the best next move is to restore the previous scheduler settings that matched the strongest visible result before trying a different direction.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimization and data-exposure settings now look much stronger than the original head capacity. The current classifier bottleneck is very narrow relative to the flattened feature map, so widening the hidden layer is a simple low-risk capacity increase with little effect on training dynamics or runtime.","secondary_modes":["micro","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 192","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The previous head-width increase helped materially, which suggests the classifier is still the main capacity bottleneck. Widening it again is a cheap way to test whether more of the remaining error is coming from an overly compressed projection rather than the convolutional trunk.","secondary_modes":["micro","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The classifier widening continues to help, which suggests the model can use more representational capacity overall. The next simple test is to strengthen the convolutional trunk itself by increasing the base channel width, keeping the same overall training recipe and architecture pattern.","secondary_modes":["micro","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The stronger width and head settings indicate the model is still benefiting from more capacity. Increasing depth by one block is a natural next step: it adds hierarchical feature extraction and one more downsampling stage while actually shrinking the classifier input width, so it is a meaningful architecture change without an uncontrolled parameter explosion.","secondary_modes":["micro","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The jump from a deeper trunk suggests the model is still capacity-limited in its convolutional representation. A moderate increase in base channels is the simplest next extension of the winning direction, and should strengthen all three stages without changing the training recipe.","secondary_modes":["micro","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 192","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The trunk has continued to benefit from more capacity, and the current three-stage feature extractor now feeds a reasonably rich representation into a still-moderate hidden head. Increasing the classifier width is a low-risk follow-on that should add capacity with much less runtime cost than widening the convolutional stack again.","secondary_modes":["micro","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-3","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The model has accumulated substantial capacity in both trunk and head, while training now covers the dataset multiple times within budget. A moderate increase in weight decay is a simple way to test whether validation loss is now limited more by overfitting than by under-capacity.","secondary_modes":["layout","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The stronger weight decay slightly worsened validation loss, so the highest-confidence next move is to restore the previous regularization setting before spending the remaining budget on a different direction.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The model has become substantially larger than the configuration that originally motivated the aggressive 4e-3 setting. With more capacity and multiple effective passes over the data, a slightly lower peak learning rate is a plausible way to trade some early aggressiveness for a lower final validation loss.","secondary_modes":["summaries","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The lower learning rate lost ground, so the safest next action is to restore the stronger 4e-3 setting that matched the best visible result before using the final budget on any further exploration.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 384","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The classifier-width increases have remained beneficial even after the trunk was strengthened, and this is one of the cheapest remaining ways to add capacity. With only two steps left, another head-width increase is a focused, low-risk attempt to capture a bit more of the gain trend without materially disturbing the rest of the winning recipe.","secondary_modes":["micro","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The final width increase slightly worsened validation loss, so the highest-confidence last action is to restore the previous 256-unit head, which matches the best visible configuration.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}
